entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: GOING TO LAW.
entries.body: The Corinthians were wrong in two ways. First, they were unfaithful, and second, they were expounding God’s laws with a show of respect but in reality attributing their authority to idols. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: SUBMITTING TO UNBELIEVERS.
entries.body: Paul says that Christians should not submit their disputes to outside arbitration. For how can it be anything other than absurd for a man who disagrees with his friend to choose their mutual enemy as their reconciler? How can you avoid feeling shame when a pagan sits in judgment on a Christian? And if it is not right to go to law before pagans about private matters, how can we submit other things of greater importance to them for a decision? Note too how Paul speaks. He calls the pagans not unbelievers but unrighteous, and the Christians he calls saints, using the appropriate description in order to deter them from getting involved with the secular courts. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.4

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: NO CHRISTIAN JUDGES AT THE TIME.
entries.body: This proves that there were no Christian judges at that time, because Paul refers to them all as unrighteous. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 6

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: AVOID SCANDAL.
entries.body: Paul did not want them to be judged by outsiders because he did not want the shortcomings of those who had been taught propriety and righteousness to become a scandal to those outside the church. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: UNBELIEF WILL BE FINALLY JUDGED.
entries.body: The saints will judge this world because the unbelief of the world will be condemned by the example of their faith. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: VIEW TRIVIAL MATTERS IN THE PERSPECTIVE OF THE LAST DAY.
entries.body: It is a disgrace for Christians to be judged by outsiders over trivial matters, when we shall judge them in far more important things. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.5

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: WHO WILL JUDGE?
entries.body: The twelve apostles will judge the twelve tribes of Israel, if they have not believed and for that reason rejected Christ. The other saints, that is to say, the Gentiles, will judge those who have not abandoned idols and believed in the true God. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BELIEVERS ARE TO JUDGE ANGELS.
entries.body: Angels are to be judged by us in the same way as the world is to be judged. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: WHETHER DEMONS WILL BE JUDGED.
entries.body: Some people say that Paul was thinking of corrupt priests when he said this, but that cannot be right. He is really talking about the demons. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.5

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: FALSE TEACHERS TO BE JUDGED.
entries.body: Paul is not talking here about real angels but about the priests and teachers of the people who will be judged by the saints because of their false teaching about Christ. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: ARE THESE DEMONS WHO WERE ONCE ANGELS?
entries.body: By angels here, Paul means demons who once were angels. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 195

entries.author: Cassiodorus
entries.title: PARTICIPATING WITH CHRIST IN JUDGMENT.
entries.body: Who can conceive of such a miracle? Who can mentally grasp such great glory? . . . All participate with Christ in judgment who do not oppose his commands, for with the devoted they too will share in his decisions. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: EVEN THE LOWLIEST BELIEVER.
entries.body: The lowest person in the church is preferable in judgment to an unbeliever. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THOSE LEAST ESTEEMED.
entries.body: Therefore the apostle wished wise, holy, and faithful persons who were well established in the various places to be judges of such matters, and not persons who, in preaching, traveled about here and there. . . . If wise judges were lacking, he wished even the lowly and contemptible to be appointed so that the affairs of Christians might not be brought to the public eye. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: SHAME THAT THERE ARE SO FEW WISE.
entries.body: Paul attacks the Corinthians because, although they are right in the middle of Greece, they have no truly wise people in their midst, even though many had gone to preach wisdom to them. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: SOME BELIEVERS ARE WISE ENOUGH.
entries.body: Paul meant that they were so unmanageable and thoughtless that they might choose inexperienced brothers as judges. There must, he said, be some people in the church wise enough to judge such cases, and they should be allowed to do so. He said this, incidentally, because at that time there was no official leader in their church. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: SETTLING DISPUTES AMONG CHRISTIANS.
entries.body: We have rulers of the church to whom we should take our disputes, so that we will not be summoned before the law courts of unbelievers. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: TAKING BELIEVERS TO THE COURTS OF UNBELIEVERS DOUBLY WRONG.
entries.body: There is a double sin here. First, they were taking each other to court, and secondly, they were going to court before unbelievers. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 6

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE NEED FOR UNDERSTANDING AND DISCERNMENT.
entries.body: When brothers go to law against each other, there is no need for a mediator to show understanding and discernment. Brotherly feeling and relationship contribute greatly toward the settlement of such quarrels. To take such a quarrel before unbelievers merely makes everything that much worse and prevents a happy resolution of the problem. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.6

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: IMPLIES NO DISRESPECT FOR CIVIL AUTHORITIES.
entries.body: This in no way contradicts Romans [13], where Paul tells people to respect the magistrates. He is not telling us to resist secular authorities, but rather we should not appeal to them. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 195

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BRING THE CASE TO THE CHURCH.
entries.body: A Christian ought not to engage in litigation at all, but if the matter is too serious to be disregarded he should bring the case to the church, so as not to incur an immediate penalty and personal downfall. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Basil the Great
entries.title: PREVENTING EVIL CONSEQUENCES.
entries.body: In this manner we shall save our adversary also, even against his will, from evil consequences, and we ourselves will not violate the commandment of God, being as his ministers neither contentious nor avaricious, steadily intent upon the manifestation of truth and never overstepping the appointed limits of zeal. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: HANDLING CONFLICT WITH RESTRAINT.
entries.body: The just man handles everything with restraint, demonstrating the remarkable degree of his own good sense and teaching not only those present at the time but also everyone in the future never to settle our differences with our relatives by feuding. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: WHAT WERE THEY DOING?
entries.body: By saying all this Paul is showing that the Corinthians were doing the exact opposite. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 196

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: DEFRAUDING BROTHERS.
entries.body: Paul is rebuking the people whose wrongful behavior has started the quarrels. Not only are they liable to be charged for the fraud which they have committed; they also share in the fault of those who, compelled by their injurious or fraudulent actions, call upon unbelievers to pass judgment. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: MULTIPLYING GUILT.
entries.body: Here there may be as many as four crimes involved. The first is not knowing how to bear being wronged. The second is to do wrong. The third is to reserve settlement of the matter to the unrighteous. And the fourth is that this kind of thing is being done to a fellow believer. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.7

entries.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch
entries.title: ADULTERY CORRUPTS WHOLE FAMILIES.
entries.body: Do not be deceived, my brothers. Corrupters of houses will not inherit the kingdom of God. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: SEX BELONGS WITHIN MARRIAGE.
entries.body: Let no one say: I was young. Before I got married, I slept with prostitutes. Why did you not get married instead? {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: HARDER TO EXCUSE KNOWING SIN.
entries.body: Paul indicates that they are not sinning unknowingly, and so it is that much harder to excuse them. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: NO HOME FOR IMMORALITY IN THE KINGDOM.
entries.body: The kingdom of God must be purified of all sin and immorality, so that God may reign in it. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: READINESS FOR THE KINGDOM.
entries.body: Paul did not say this because the Corinthians did not know it already. He was rather reawakening their reverence for the divine commandment and bringing them closer to readiness for the kingdom of heaven. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: CONVICTING ALL.
entries.body: Paul does not confine his accusations to a short list of types of sin but condemns all equally. He is not so much getting at particular sins as making a general admonition that will secretly convict anyone who may have such things on his conscience. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.8

entries.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
entries.title: ONLY TWO PLACES TO GO ETERNALLY.
entries.body: If someone says that he does not want the kingdom of God, only eternal rest, he should not deceive himself. For there are only two places, and not a third. If a person does not deserve to reign with Christ, then he will most assuredly perish with the devil. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WASHED FROM ALL SIN.
entries.body: The Corinthians had received all the benefits of purity in their baptism, which is the foundation of the truth of the gospel. In baptism the believer is washed clean from all sins and is made righteous in the name of the Lord, and through the Spirit of God he is adopted as God’s child. With these words, Paul is reminding them how great and how special is the grace which they have received in the true tradition. But afterward, by thinking which is contrary to this baptismal rule of faith, they had stripped themselves of these benefits. For this reason he is trying to bring them back to their original way of thinking, so that they can recover what they had once received. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: AVOID SIN AFTER BAPTISM.
entries.body: The Corinthians were not to bother about the sins they committed before baptism. All that should concern them now was that they should not sin again in the future. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 6

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: COUNTLESS BLESSINGS.
entries.body: Paul says this to make the Corinthians feel ashamed of themselves. He asks them to think about the great evils from which God had delivered them. But God did not limit his salvation to mere deliverance. He greatly extended the benefit by making them clean, by going on to make them holy and finally by making them righteous in his sight. Even bare deliverance from our sins would have been a great gift, but God has gone on from that to fill us with countless blessings. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.9

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: CHANGED FOR THE BETTER.
entries.body: Paul says that they have been changed for the better, not so as to lose concupiscence altogether, a condition never realized in this life, but so as to not obey the desire to sin. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch
entries.body: Those that corrupt families shall not inherit the kingdom of God [1 Cor 6:9–10]. If, then, those who do this as respects the flesh have suffered death, how much more shall this be true of anyone who corrupts by wicked doctrine the faith of God, for which Jesus Christ was crucified! Anyone becoming defiled [in this way], shall go away into everlasting fire, and so shall everyone who listens unto him.

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: PREMISED ON SELF-DISCIPLINE.
entries.body: All things are lawful, but that is obviously premised upon self-discipline. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: QUALIFYING “ALL THINGS.”
entries.body: By all things Paul presumably means those things which are contained in the natural law and which were also lawful for his fellow apostles. It would not refer to the law of Moses, because Moses forbade many things owing to the hardness of heart of an unbelieving and stiff-necked people. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: AVOID SLAVERY TO DESIRES.
entries.body: Paul means that if we are free to choose, then we should remain free and not become a slave to any particular desire. Anyone who orders his desires properly remains the master of them, but once he goes beyond this limit he loses control and becomes their slave. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 161.1

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: SOME CHOICES ARE FLATLY WRONG.
entries.body: Now that we are no longer under the law, we have the freedom to make choices, but we need to realize that some choices are right and others wrong. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 19.7

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: FOODS WILL COME TO NOTHING.
entries.body: We must restrain the belly and keep it under the control of heaven. God will finally bring to nothing all that is made for the belly, as the apostle says. {fn:0}

entries.author: Novatian
entries.title: PRETENDING THE BELLY IS GOD.
entries.body: One who worships God through food is almost like one who has God for his belly. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: RULING THE BODY.
entries.body: The body, being dedicated to God, will be rewarded with a spiritual reward for the merit of its ruler, which is the rational soul. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: NOT AN ATTACK ON THE BODY.
entries.body: Paul is not attacking the nature of the body but the unbridled license of the mind, which abuses the body. The body was not made for the purpose of fornication, nor was it created for gluttony. It was meant to have Christ as its head, so that it might follow him. We should be overcome with shame and horror-struck if we defile ourselves with such great evils, once we have been accounted worthy of the great honor of being members of him who sits on high. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 17.1

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: CHRIST’S RISEN BODY AND OUR BODIES.
entries.body: If our body is a member of Christ and Christ has risen from the dead, our body will surely follow his lead. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 16.2

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: HE WILL ALSO RAISE US.
entries.body: Paul did not write this because of Christ’s resurrection, which had already taken place, but because of ours, so that we might believe and silence our opponents. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: DO NOT DEMEAN YOUR BODY.
entries.body: Paul seeks to shame the fornicator by saying that if he really belongs to Christ he ought to know better than to indulge in such demeaning behavior. He speaks in graphic terms about the prostitute in order to startle his hearers and fill them with alarm. Nothing could be better suited to strike them with horror than this expression. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 17.1

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: RISE WITH HIM.
entries.body: You are all members of Christ because you have been united to him by being born again of the Spirit. You have the hope that you will rise again, just as he rose. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: FORNICATION ROOTED IN THE FLESH.
entries.body: Other sins like anger and greed come from the soul, but fornication has its roots in the flesh. Paul makes special mention of it here because it was the problem which he had to deal with at the time. Fornication is not self-evidently the worst sin of all. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: UNITED WITH A PROSTITUTE.
entries.body: Paul says this because the person who involves himself in contamination is united with the person with whom he involves himself. Sexual immorality makes them both one,{fn:0} in nature as well as in sin. {fn:1}

entries.author: Oecumenius
entries.title: THE TWO ARE ONE FLESH.
entries.body: A man guilty of fornication and the impurity which comes from it insults his marriage and his wife. He sins against his own body and consequently against his wife, because the two are one flesh. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: ONE SPIRIT WITH HIM.
entries.body: The soul of Jesus clung to God from the beginning of the creation in a union inseparable and indissoluble, as the soul of the wisdom and word of God, and of the truth and the true light. Receiving him wholly and itself entering into his light and splendor, it was made one spirit with him in a preeminent degree. This is what the apostle promises to those who imitate Jesus. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE SPIRIT SHARED.
entries.body: The Spirit of God is shared between God and human beings when we are united to the Lord. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: SHUN VILE SERVITUDE.
entries.body: Only by a swift flight can we shun the savagery of such a rabid mistress and escape from such vile servitude. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: SINNING AGAINST BODY AND SOUL.
entries.body: If someone hangs himself or kills himself with a dagger, he does not sin against his body but against his soul, on which he inflicts violence. But to fornicate is a sin of the body which touches both the body and the soul. {fn:0}

entries.author: Pelagius
entries.title: COMPOUNDING OTHERS’ SINS.
entries.body: Fornication multiplies sins because two people are involved, and both perish together. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 6

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE ENTIRE BODY DEFILED.
entries.body: What Paul says here obviously applies to the murderer, the covetous person and the extortioner equally well. But as it was not possible to mention anything worse than fornication, Paul magnifies the crime by saying that in the fornicator the entire body is defiled. It is a sin against one’s own self in a way that the others are not. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 18.2

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: BE AWARE OF THE PERNICIOUSNESS OF EVIL.
entries.body: Note that Paul did not say that we should hate immorality, but that we should shun it as people who are aware of the perniciousness of evil. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 198

entries.author: Anonymous Syriac Author
entries.title: A TEMPLE AND AN ALTAR.
entries.body: The body and heart in which our Lord dwells—also because the Spirit resides there—is in truth a temple and an altar, seeing that our Lord resides there. {fn:0}

entries.author: Tertullian
entries.title: THE BODY NOT A PRISON BUT A TEMPLE.
entries.body: In the Platonic view, the body is a prison; in that of Paul, it is the temple of God because it is in Christ. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: YOU ARE NOT YOUR OWN.
entries.body: Paul said this with the intention that we should keep our bodies uncontaminated, so that the Holy Spirit may dwell in them. {fn:0}

entries.author: Severian of Gabala
entries.title: CORRUPTING THE TEMPLE.
entries.body: The fornicator is also guilty of impiety, for by doing harm to his body he has corrupted the temple of the Holy Spirit. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: BRING NOTHING DEFILED INTO THE TEMPLE.
entries.body: Let us glorify God and bear him in a pure and spotless body and with more perfect observance. Let those who have been redeemed by the blood of Christ submit to the rule of our redeemer with the absolute obedience of servants. Let us take care not to bring anything unclean or defiled into the temple of God, lest he be offended and leave the abode where he dwells. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: BOUGHT WITH A PRICE.
entries.body: Someone who has been bought does not have the power to make decisions, but the person who bought him does. And because we were bought for a very high price, we ought to serve our master all the more, so that the offense from which he has bought our release may not turn us back over to death. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: WE ARE NOT OUR OWN.
entries.body: What is Paul trying to prove when he says that we are not our own? He wants to secure us against sin and against following the improper desires of the mind. We have many improper desires, but we must constrain them, and we can do so. If we could not, there would be no point in exhorting us like this. Paul does not say that we are under compulsion but that we have been bought—and bought with a great price, reminding us of the way in which our salvation was obtained. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 18.3
